speaker: interviewer
text: With Stella Faitek, on March the 6th, 1977.

speaker: interviewer
text: '97 [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. March the 6th, 1997. [LAUGHTER] In terms of just going back to the children, and your family, both of your children are-- are now married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their wives are-- Mark's wife is--

speaker: interviewee
text: A nurse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or let's start with Leon. Leon's wife is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Is a teacher of special education.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Judy Faitek. Mark's wife is a nurse, registered nurse. They have three children, two children their own, one adopted from Paraguay. Leon works for the Navy. They have very good jobs, very good situated. And I am proud of my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you should be. And-- and how old is your oldest grandchild now?

speaker: interviewee
text: 16 going onto 17. And David is 13 going on 14. And the little Isabella is 8. She is adopted from Paraguay, a very smart girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to the United States, did you join a synagogue of any kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I belong to B'nai Shalom, here, [? Batala ?] shul. And except this, I have the shul in the Northwest home. The rabbi knows me very well. So I have here the shul and here the shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your children, your sons went to Hebrew school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which Hebrew school did they go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mark went to Hebrew school in [? Sawyer. ?] I don't remember the rabbi's name. Mark went to Arbeter Ring Workmen's Circle School. And they finished the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Leon also went to Arbeter Ring?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leon to Arbeter Ring. Mark went to the shul where I lived in [? Sawyer. ?] B'nai Shalom I think it was. And Leon belongs to the shul in San Diego. He belongs to the choir in the shul. He works for the Navy. He has a lots of rewards. So does Judy. They are very active in San Diego.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the awards are for with help-- helping hemophilia, in the Hemophilia Foundation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was the president in Hemophilia Foundation. Now he's the vice president, because he was working too much. And Judy is the secretary there. Judy helped much the Hemophilia Foundation. She was collecting money. Last year she did make $48,000 for them, Hemophilia Foundation, from which they opened a clinic for the Hemophiliacs. They are two beautiful kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mark, is Mark associated or affiliated with the synagogue? He lives in Minneapolis.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not very much. She is a Catholic. She is not Jewish. So he is not going to synagogue. And she's not going to church. So they're medium-- in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: His children-- a bar mitzvah for his children, or no?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But Adam now works for the JCC, the Jewish organization. He helps. He donated his time. And sometimes he's getting paid something. But most of the time he's volunteering.

speaker: interviewer
text: Looking back on your life, with focusing on Dr. Korczak, what influence do you feel that he had on you as a person?

speaker: interviewee
text: I thank him for being what I am, thanks to him. He gave me the knowledge. He gave me the possibility. He raised me. I feel that I have a lot from his education. I earned from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you hear about what happened to Dr. Korczak during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: With him was one of his raised children. All the time he used to bring him food. Because Dr. Korczak developed-- I think he had diabetes. I think so. And he helped him with diet. He helped him with everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto. He also went to the oven together with Stepha, with the kids, and Dr.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you hear about that? It was after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the book. From the book I heard it. Because I was here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't know what happened to him until you came here and read about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went to Poland to a conference, that at the conference were children who were in Dr. Korczak's homes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. They came mostly from Israel. From Israel they were a group, I think, 20. Then one student which practiced by Korczak came from Australia. One of the boys which were in ghetto, with Korczak, they went out to the mother to visit the mother, to another place. In the meantime, they took the children with Dr. Korczak to the ghetto, the ovens. The two boys were too late. One of the boys, is alive in Paris, in Fra-- Fra-- France. I think that he's now in Israel living. But one of the two boys is alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember most about the conference in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to the conference came my girlfriend, which lives in Germany. We were talking about how life was there. And visiting the people which came to-- from Israel to Poland, it was for me a big surprise. Because I didn't know that they are coming. It was-- everything was very surprisingly. They were very nice to us. They did everything what we want them to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the Poles?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, yeah. And those people are working still with the home. There-- there is our home, in Korczak's home. There are still orphans-- but not Jewish-- the orphans.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you still have a cousin in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, in Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he is-- he does not-- does he identify as a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. He's a Jew. He married a Polish lady, which is very nice, very good to him. She's a very nice person. I like her very much. I correspond with him. I send packages to them. I sent a lot of packages to Russia, to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because in Russia you have other relatives?

speaker: interviewee
text: No relatives-- people who saved my life. The Emma, which is the fiddler, she has a daughter, which is a translator from Russian to English. She finished English school. She writes beautiful English. She's alive. Then her aunt's son, the little boy which was born, I told you, 19-- the 26th of April, her sisters-- her aunt's boy, is also in Moscow. I help them. And the other Emma, which is in Gorky.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who-- who is that Emma, in Gorky?

speaker: interviewee
text: Emma's daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Emma's daughter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Emma's daughter. Her name is Maya.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: She lives there too. I send packages to them, and in Moscow, and in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you feel that Dr. Korczak can teach people about how to treat other people? What is the-- what do you think-- What do you think people can learn from Dr. Korczak as far as how to treat others?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a hard question to answer. To learn from him, everything is to learn from him-- kindness, forgiveness, goodness, help, helpness. There is a note from him before he went to ovens. Poland, the world as it is, has no right to exist. These were his last words before he went to ovens. He see what is going on. He told-- the children didn't know where they are going. When they marched to the ovens, to the trains, with a flag, he'd told them that they are going to a parade. They didn't know that they are going to the ovens. So to learn from him, it's too much to talk about-- everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about-- what can we learn from him about how to treat children, how to raise children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Give them freedom, kindness. Don't be too soft. Have a arm, a hand. And the children know what they are doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as far as something, a message, that you would-- would like to convey out of all of your experiences, what have-- what have you learned about life and about people and--

speaker: interviewee
text: What I learned is to understand people, to like people, give a little, take a little, forgive for somebody which was not nice to you, forgive him, forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as far as the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope it will never happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. I think I-- that is something, a feeling, I think that we all share.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the-- the whole world should think like this. It should never happen to anybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much--

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for the interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: We very much appreciate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks. This is Dr. Janusz Korczak before the war. This is-- I think it's his last picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Stefania Wilczynski, the right hand of Dr. Korczak.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture I got from Israel. My cousin used to work with Korczak, and she was corresponding. And she gave it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of the dining room, 15 tables. It's the original picture [BACKGROUND NOISE] in Korczak's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture I got-- from before the war I got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you at one time, you were at one of those tables.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes I was at this table, table 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right in the front?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The front table. And what is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the group of sport boys which were taking part in the games, volleyball and basketball.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was in your orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In Korczak's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Korczak's home. And the man in the front row?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is Mr. [? Buchner ?] the teacher of sport-- sport instructor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was also your teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is in summer camp, with a group of the older children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the center?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the center is Dr. Korczak, in the middle. And below him is the [? Buchner, ?] the sport instructor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you also went to summer camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but here are only boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is the representation for going to Israel, for the [? Maccabiah ?].

speaker: interviewer
text: Which you never went to.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are in the middle of the first row.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am in the middle, sitting in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to the right of you, with her--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sara Bromberg, she's alive in Israel. She is an eye doctor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to the left of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my wedding, the picture I made with the children I raised in the orphanage. And on sides are my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to the right of you, as we're looking at the picture, to the right of you, that person is alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [? Niete ?] [? Greenbaum, ?] he's alive living in Israel. I saw him 2 years ago. I don't know what's going on now. I hope that he's alive. So they are from my wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: And anybody else that is alive, or no?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nobody's alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my wedding, 1939, April the 20th.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're standing in front of a synagogue? There's some Hebrew writing in the background.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall which synagogue it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in rabbi's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was in the rabbi's home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rabbi's home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture is made in Warsaw, before the war, with my two children, which I saved. I took them out from Warsaw. And I placed them in Bialystok, in an orphanage. And behind me is the helper, my helper to raise my children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. The other's is--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's the one on left?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the right as we're looking at the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left is [INAUDIBLE]. I don't remember his last name. This is my best friend. I met her in Russia, which saved my life. Her name is Emma Borisovna Rivchun. She was the first fiddler in Moscow Theater, which saved our life. Thank you, Emma.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at the time, you did not know she was Jewish. But later you learned that she was Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know that she is Jewish. But later when I start writing to her letters, I found out that she is Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Warsaw, 1989, I think, at the conference where Korczak meeting, to which came people from all around the world to this conference. I don't know those people. This is my son, his wife, Chris.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is Mark then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mark.

speaker: interviewer
text: And let's just go from left to right. So first we have Mark, and next to Mark is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Adam.

speaker: interviewer
text: Adam. And next to Adam is--

speaker: interviewee
text: Chris, Mark's wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Isabella.

speaker: interviewer
text: The adopted--

speaker: interviewee
text: The adopted little girl. And this is Erica, the oldest. She's a marvelous swimmer. I thought that she will be in the Olympics this year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long ago was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was probably taken about 5 years ago, 4 or 5 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Who is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Leon, with this wife, Angel Judy Faitek. This picture is made in California, in San Diego.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is a recent picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is about 4 years ago. This is my brother, Sam, about 10 years ago with my son Leon. Leon was very much in love with my brother. He raised him. Kaddish all year. And every year he remembers. He goes to shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that is Sam or Joe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sam. He raised Leon. He sacrificed his life.
